Idiom (Pijin): Taem solwota i drae bae yu lukim ston
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional clause proverb structure (taem + clause + future marker bae)
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Truth or hidden problems eventually become visible over time
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain Rephrasi: Eventually the truth will become clear
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Island proverb referencing low tide revealing stones in lagoons across Solomon Islands
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Pijin): /taem ˈsolwɔta i drae bae ju ˈlukim ston/
First Example Sentence: Taem olketa audit olketa kam, taem solwota i drae bae yu lukim ston.
Second Example Sentence: Mi talem olketa finis se taem solwota i drae bae yu lukim ston.
Third Example Sentence: Long olketa case olsem, taem solwota i drae bae yu lukim ston.

Idiom (Pijin): Wan hand nomoa i no save mekem bigfala noise
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative declarative proverb with modal save
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: One person alone cannot achieve something large
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common Melanesian communal value emphasizing cooperation
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Pijin): /wan han noˈmoa i no save ˈmekem ˈbigfala nɔiz/
First Example Sentence: Long project ia, wan hand nomoa i no save mekem bigfala noise.
Second Example Sentence: Boss talem mifala se wan hand nomoa i no save mekem bigfala noise.
Third Example Sentence: Yumi mas waka tugeta bikos wan hand nomoa i no save mekem bigfala noise.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Speaking gently calms anger and tension
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Traditional conflict-avoidance communication style in Melanesian societies
First Example Sentence: Taem olketa start kros, mi talem olketa tok isi mekem bel isi.
Second Example Sentence: Papa save talem tok isi mekem bel isi taem dispute i start.
Third Example Sentence: Teacher remindim olketa student se tok isi mekem bel isi.

Idiom (Pijin): Wind i tanem sail blong boat
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from seafaring culture across Pacific island communities
First Example Sentence: Long election ia, wind i tanem sail blong boat.
Second Example Sentence: Mi ting plan bae succeed, bat wind i tanem sail blong boat.
Third Example Sentence: Taem olketa news i kam aot, wind i tanem sail blong boat.

Idiom (Pijin): Man wea slip wetem dog bae kasem flea
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: People who associate with bad influences suffer consequences
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Borrowed proverb structure common across English creole traditions
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Pijin): /man wea slip ˈwetem dɔg bae ˈkasem fliː/
First Example Sentence: Mi warnim hem se man wea slip wetem dog bae kasem flea.
Second Example Sentence: Taem olketa police arrestim olketa, mi tingim se man wea slip wetem dog bae kasem flea.
Third Example Sentence: Mama save talem pikinini se man wea slip wetem dog bae kasem flea.

Idiom (Pijin): Slow slow nomoa bae yumi kasem ples
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain Rephrasi: Take your time and you will succeed
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common motivational saying in Solomon Islands communities
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Pijin): /sloʊ sloʊ noˈmoa bae ˈjumi ˈkasem ples/
First Example Sentence: Taem waka i hard, mi talem olketa slow slow nomoa bae yumi kasem ples.
Second Example Sentence: Coach talem team se slow slow nomoa bae yumi kasem ples.
Third Example Sentence: Teacher encouragem olketa student se slow slow nomoa bae yumi kasem ples.

Idiom (Pijin): Bel blong man i hot olsem faea
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Based on metaphor of fire used in many Melanesian languages
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Pijin): /bel bloŋ man i hɔt ɔˈlesem ˈfaea/
First Example Sentence: Taem hem herem tok ia, bel blong man i hot olsem faea.
Second Example Sentence: Boss hem kam long ofis wetem bel blong man i hot olsem faea.
Third Example Sentence: Mi lukim se bel blong hem i hot olsem faea.

Idiom (Pijin): Pikinini blong snake hem snake tu
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Children often inherit traits from their parents
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common creole proverb referencing inherited traits
First Example Sentence: Taem hem mekem sem ting olsem papa, olketa talem pikinini blong snake hem snake tu.
Second Example Sentence: Village olketa laef taem olketa talem pikinini blong snake hem snake tu.
Third Example Sentence: Mi herem olketa joke se pikinini blong snake hem snake tu.

Idiom (Pijin): Rain bae foldaon long head blong hem
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Island imagery using sudden tropical rain as metaphor for trouble
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Pijin): /ren bae fɔlˈdaon loŋ hed bloŋ hem/
First Example Sentence: Sapos hem continue olsem, rain bae foldaon long head blong hem.
Second Example Sentence: Village olketa tok se rain bae foldaon long head blong hem.
Third Example Sentence: Mi warnim hem finis se rain bae foldaon long head blong hem.

Idiom (Pijin): Fish i save swim nomoa long wata blong hem
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: People perform best in familiar environments
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from fishing culture central to Solomon Islands livelihood
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Pijin): /fis i save swim noˈmoa loŋ ˈwata bloŋ hem/
First Example Sentence: Coach talem player se fish i save swim nomoa long wata blong hem.
Second Example Sentence: Taem hem go bak long village, fish i save swim nomoa long wata blong hem.
Third Example Sentence: Mi tingim proverb ia se fish i save swim nomoa long wata blong hem.
